items.title: Security requirements are defined and tracked
items.description: Functional and non-functional security requirements (authN/authZ, data classification, regulatory obligations) are captured as first-class backlog items, not assumed. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Secure SDLC Integration — Shift-Left Security: Confirm security requirements exist in the issue tracker, are linked to user stories, and have acceptance criteria reviewed before development starts
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for security requirements are defined and tracked, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Threat modeling is performed during design
items.description: New features and architectural changes undergo structured threat modeling (e.g., STRIDE) to identify trust boundaries, attack surfaces, and mitigations before code is written. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Secure SDLC Integration — Shift-Left Security: Verify threat models exist for major components, are updated on significant design changes, and produce tracked mitigation items
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for threat modeling is performed during design, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Security gates block insecure promotions
items.description: CI/CD pipelines enforce policy gates (failing builds on critical findings, required reviews, signed artifacts) that prevent code from advancing without meeting a security bar. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Secure SDLC Integration — Shift-Left Security: Confirm pipeline configuration fails on critical/high findings, that bypass requires documented approval, and that gates cannot be silently disabled
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for security gates block insecure promotions, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Security design reviews are mandatory for high-risk changes
items.description: High-blast-radius changes (auth, crypto, payment, multi-tenant boundaries) require a documented security design review before implementation proceeds. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Secure SDLC Integration — Shift-Left Security: Verify a trigger list exists for mandatory reviews and that recent high-risk PRs show evidence of completed security design review
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for security design reviews are mandatory for high-risk changes, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

sections.title: Secure SDLC Integration — Developer Enablement & Training

items.title: Secure coding training is delivered and tracked
items.description: Developers receive role-relevant secure coding training (OWASP Top 10, language-specific pitfalls) with completion tracked and refreshed periodically. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Secure SDLC Integration — Developer Enablement & Training: Confirm training records exist, cover relevant languages/frameworks, and are refreshed at least annually with measurable completion rates
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for secure coding training is delivered and tracked, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: A security champions program exists
items.description: Each delivery team has an embedded security champion who reviews designs, triages findings, and acts as the local liaison to the central security team. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find: Secure SDLC Integration — Developer Enablement & Training: Verify champions are named per team, have defined responsibilities and time allocation, and meet regularly with the security function
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for a security champions program exists, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Secure coding standards and golden paths are published
items.description: The organization publishes secure coding standards, hardened libraries, and paved-road templates so developers default to safe patterns. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Secure SDLC Integration — Developer Enablement & Training: Confirm coding standards exist, reference approved crypto/auth libraries, and that paved-road scaffolds embed security controls by default
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for secure coding standards and golden paths are published, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Security findings drive targeted education
items.description: Recurring vulnerability classes from scanning and pentests feed back into training content and guidance, closing the knowledge gap that produced them. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find: Secure SDLC Integration — Developer Enablement & Training: Verify there is a mechanism linking top recurring finding categories to updated guidance, lunch-and-learns, or remediation playbooks
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for security findings drive targeted education, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

sections.title: Automated Security Testing — Static Application Security Testing (SAST)

items.title: SAST runs automatically in the pipeline
items.description: Static analysis executes on every pull request and merge, scanning all in-scope repositories rather than running only ad hoc. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Automated Security Testing — Static Application Security Testing (SAST): Confirm SAST is wired into CI for all primary repos, runs on PRs, and posts results back to the developer in-context
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for sast runs automatically in the pipeline, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: A SAST baseline separates new from legacy findings
items.description: A committed baseline allows the pipeline to fail only on newly introduced issues, avoiding alert fatigue from a legacy backlog while still tracking it. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Automated Security Testing — Static Application Security Testing (SAST): Verify a baseline file exists, is reviewed periodically, and that net-new critical findings break the build while legacy debt is tracked separately
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for a sast baseline separates new from legacy findings, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: False positives are managed with auditable suppressions
items.description: Suppressions and rule tuning are version-controlled, justified, and reviewed, so the tool stays trustworthy and suppression is not abused to hide real risk. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Automated Security Testing — Static Application Security Testing (SAST): Confirm suppressions live in code with documented rationale, expire or are re-reviewed, and that suppression rates are monitored
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for false positives are managed with auditable suppressions, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: SAST ruleset coverage matches the tech stack
items.description: Enabled rulesets cover every primary language and framework in use, including security-relevant categories like injection, deserialization, and SSRF. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Automated Security Testing — Static Application Security Testing (SAST): Verify enabled rule packs map to all languages/frameworks in the codebase and that high-impact categories are not disabled
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for sast ruleset coverage matches the tech stack, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

sections.title: Automated Security Testing — Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST)

items.title: DAST scans run automatically against deployed builds
items.description: Dynamic scans execute against a staging/test deployment as part of the pipeline, catching runtime issues SAST cannot see. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Automated Security Testing — Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST): Confirm DAST runs against a representative environment per release candidate and that results are routed into the tracking workflow
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for dast scans run automatically against deployed builds, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Authenticated and API scanning is configured
items.description: DAST is configured with valid sessions and API specifications so it reaches authenticated surfaces and exercises real endpoints, not just public pages. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Automated Security Testing — Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST): Verify the scanner has working auth (session/token) and is fed OpenAPI/GraphQL schemas to maximize coverage of protected functionality
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for authenticated and api scanning is configured, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: DAST runs against safe, non-production targets
items.description: Active scanning is pointed at dedicated test environments with seeded data to avoid disrupting production or corrupting live data. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Automated Security Testing — Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST): Confirm scan targets are non-production, that destructive checks are constrained, and that production scanning (if any) is explicitly authorized and passive
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for dast runs against safe, non-production targets, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: DAST findings are deduplicated and triaged
items.description: Results are correlated across scans to reduce noise, and confirmed issues are tracked to remediation with severity-based SLAs. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find: Automated Security Testing — Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST): Verify findings flow into a vulnerability management tool, duplicates are merged, and confirmed issues have owners and due dates
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for dast findings are deduplicated and triaged, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

sections.title: Automated Security Testing — Software Composition Analysis (SCA)

items.title: Dependencies are scanned for known vulnerabilities
items.description: Every build scans direct and transitive dependencies against vulnerability databases and fails or flags on critical CVEs. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Automated Security Testing — Software Composition Analysis (SCA): Confirm SCA runs on all repos including transitive dependencies, and that critical/high CVEs are surfaced with fix versions
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for dependencies are scanned for known vulnerabilities, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: OWASP Top Ten — Vulnerable and Outdated Components

items.title: License compliance is enforced
items.description: SCA evaluates dependency licenses against an allow/deny policy to prevent introduction of incompatible or restrictive licenses. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find: Automated Security Testing — Software Composition Analysis (SCA): Verify a license policy exists, that builds flag disallowed licenses, and that exceptions are documented and approved
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for license compliance is enforced, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: SCA prioritizes reachable, exploitable vulnerabilities
items.description: Findings are prioritized by exploitability (reachability, EPSS, KEV) so teams remediate truly risky issues first rather than chasing every CVE. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Automated Security Testing — Software Composition Analysis (SCA): Confirm prioritization uses reachability and/or CISA KEV/EPSS signals, and that remediation effort tracks against exploitable issues
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for sca prioritizes reachable, exploitable vulnerabilities, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: OWASP Top Ten — Vulnerable and Outdated Components

items.title: Automated dependency updates are enabled
items.description: Automated update tooling proposes upgrades for vulnerable and outdated dependencies, keeping the dependency surface current with minimal toil. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find: Automated Security Testing — Software Composition Analysis (SCA): Verify automated update PRs are enabled across repos, are reviewed and merged regularly, and that stale critical updates are not ignored
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for automated dependency updates are enabled, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Pre-commit secret scanning is in place
items.description: Developer machines run pre-commit hooks that block credentials, keys, and tokens before they ever reach the remote repository. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Automated Security Testing — Secret Scanning: Confirm pre-commit secret hooks are installed across teams and that they block (not just warn) on detected secrets
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for pre-commit secret scanning is in place, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Pipeline secret scanning enforces a backstop
items.description: CI scans every push and PR for secrets as a server-side backstop that does not depend on developers enabling local hooks. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Automated Security Testing — Secret Scanning: Verify CI secret scanning runs on all repos and pushes, fails the build on verified secrets, and cannot be skipped without approval
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for pipeline secret scanning enforces a backstop, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Repository history is scanned for leaked secrets
items.description: Full git history is periodically scanned because secrets removed from HEAD may still be retrievable in prior commits. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Automated Security Testing — Secret Scanning: Confirm historical scans run on all repos and that any discovered secrets are treated as compromised and rotated
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for repository history is scanned for leaked secrets, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Detected secrets trigger immediate rotation
items.description: A defined process treats any exposed secret as compromised: revoke, rotate, and review for abuse, regardless of repository visibility. Treat failures here as release blockers.
items.how_to_find: Automated Security Testing — Secret Scanning: Verify a documented rotation runbook exists, has measurable response times, and that recent detections show evidence of rotation
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for detected secrets trigger immediate rotation, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Secrets are stored in a centralized secrets manager
items.description: Application and infrastructure secrets live in a dedicated secrets manager with access control and audit logging, not in source, env files, or wikis. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Secrets Management — Centralized Secrets Store: Confirm a central secrets store is the source of truth, access is least-privilege, and all access is audit-logged
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for secrets are stored in a centralized secrets manager, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Dynamic, short-lived secrets are used where possible
items.description: Databases, cloud APIs, and services receive dynamically generated, short-TTL credentials instead of long-lived static keys. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Secrets Management — Centralized Secrets Store: Verify dynamic secret engines or short-lived tokens are used for high-value backends and that TTLs are minimized
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for dynamic, short-lived secrets are used where possible, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Static secrets are rotated automatically
items.description: Any unavoidable long-lived secrets are rotated on a defined schedule and on suspected compromise, with rotation automated to avoid drift. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Secrets Management — Centralized Secrets Store: Confirm rotation schedules exist, rotation is automated for supported secrets, and last-rotated timestamps are monitored
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for static secrets are rotated automatically, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Workloads authenticate via identity, not embedded keys
items.description: Services retrieve secrets using platform workload identity (e.g., cloud IAM roles, Kubernetes service accounts) rather than bootstrapping with embedded credentials. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Secrets Management — Centralized Secrets Store: Verify workloads use identity-based auth to the secrets store and that no bootstrap secrets are baked into images or manifests
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for workloads authenticate via identity, not embedded keys, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Pipeline secrets are masked and stored securely
items.description: CI secrets are stored as protected/masked variables or pulled from the secrets manager at runtime, never committed to the repository. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Secrets Management — Pipeline Secret Hygiene: Confirm CI variables holding secrets are marked masked/protected and that no secrets are present in pipeline definition files
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for pipeline secrets are masked and stored securely, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Secrets never appear in build logs or artifacts
items.description: Logging is configured to redact secrets and pipelines avoid echoing sensitive values; artifacts are inspected to ensure no credentials are baked in. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Secrets Management — Pipeline Secret Hygiene: Verify masking is active in logs, that debug modes do not leak secrets, and spot-check artifacts/images for embedded credentials
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for secrets never appear in build logs or artifacts, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Pipeline secrets are scoped and least-privilege
items.description: Secrets are scoped to specific environments, branches, and jobs so a compromised pipeline cannot reach production credentials it does not need. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Secrets Management — Pipeline Secret Hygiene: Confirm environment-scoped secrets, protected branches/environments, and that fork or PR pipelines cannot access production secrets
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for pipeline secrets are scoped and least-privilege, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Cloud access uses OIDC federation, not static keys
items.description: Pipelines authenticate to cloud providers via short-lived OIDC-federated tokens rather than long-lived static cloud access keys stored as CI secrets. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Secrets Management — Pipeline Secret Hygiene: Verify CI-to-cloud auth uses OIDC federation with scoped trust policies, and that long-lived cloud keys are not stored in the pipeline
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for cloud access uses oidc federation, not static keys, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Dependencies are pinned with committed lockfiles
items.description: Lockfiles pin exact versions and integrity hashes for all dependencies so builds are reproducible and tampering is detectable. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Supply Chain Security — Dependency Integrity: Confirm lockfiles are committed for every project, builds install from the lockfile, and CI fails if the lockfile is out of sync
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for dependencies are pinned with committed lockfiles, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: SLSA — Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts
OWASP Top Ten — Software and Data Integrity Failures

items.title: Dependency confusion is mitigated
items.description: Private package scopes and registry configuration prevent attackers from publishing a malicious public package that shadows an internal name. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Supply Chain Security — Dependency Integrity: Verify internal packages use reserved scopes/namespaces, registries are explicitly configured, and public fallback for private names is disabled
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for dependency confusion is mitigated, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: CNCF Software Supply Chain Best Practices

items.title: Dependencies are pulled through a trusted proxy
items.description: An internal artifact proxy/registry caches and vets external packages, providing a control point for blocking and integrity verification. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Supply Chain Security — Dependency Integrity: Confirm builds resolve dependencies through an internal proxy, that direct public-registry access is restricted, and that the proxy enforces policy
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for dependencies are pulled through a trusted proxy, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: CNCF Software Supply Chain Best Practices
SLSA — Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts

items.title: Dependency integrity is verified at install time
items.description: Package manager integrity checks (hash/signature verification) are enabled so corrupted or tampered packages fail the build. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Supply Chain Security — Dependency Integrity: Verify integrity/hash verification is enforced during install and that signature verification is used where the ecosystem supports it
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for dependency integrity is verified at install time, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: SLSA — Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts
OWASP Top Ten — Software and Data Integrity Failures

sections.title: Supply Chain Security — Artifact Signing & Provenance

items.title: Build artifacts are cryptographically signed
items.description: Containers and release artifacts are signed so consumers can verify authenticity and detect tampering before deployment. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Supply Chain Security — Artifact Signing & Provenance: Confirm artifacts are signed in the pipeline, signatures are stored alongside artifacts, and deploy-time verification is enabled
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for build artifacts are cryptographically signed, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: SLSA — Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts

items.title: SBOMs are generated and published for releases
items.description: A software bill of materials is produced for each artifact, enabling rapid impact analysis when a new dependency CVE is disclosed. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Supply Chain Security — Artifact Signing & Provenance: Verify SBOMs are generated per build in a standard format (SPDX/CycloneDX), stored as attestations, and queryable for incident response
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for sboms are generated and published for releases, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Build provenance attestations are produced
items.description: The pipeline emits SLSA provenance describing how, where, and from what sources an artifact was built, enabling verification of build integrity. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Supply Chain Security — Artifact Signing & Provenance: Confirm provenance attestations are generated for releases, reference the source commit and builder, and are verified before deployment
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for build provenance attestations are produced, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Signature and provenance are verified before deploy
items.description: Admission controls reject unsigned artifacts or those without valid provenance, closing the loop between signing and enforcement. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Supply Chain Security — Artifact Signing & Provenance: Verify deploy/admission policies require valid signatures and provenance, and that verification failures block rollout
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for signature and provenance are verified before deploy, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: SLSA — Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts

sections.title: Supply Chain Security — Base Image Security

items.title: Base images come from trusted, pinned sources
items.description: Base images are sourced from a vetted internal registry or trusted publisher and pinned by digest, not floating tags like latest. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Supply Chain Security — Base Image Security: Confirm base images are pinned by digest, sourced from an approved registry, and that use of arbitrary public images is restricted
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for base images come from trusted, pinned sources, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Minimal/distroless base images are used
items.description: Images are kept minimal (distroless or slim) to reduce attack surface, remove shells and package managers, and shrink the vulnerability footprint. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Supply Chain Security — Base Image Security: Verify production images are minimal, do not ship unnecessary tooling, and run as a non-root user where feasible
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for minimal/distroless base images are used, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Container images are scanned before and after publish
items.description: Images are scanned for OS and library vulnerabilities at build time and continuously in the registry as new CVEs are disclosed. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Supply Chain Security — Base Image Security: Confirm image scanning runs in the build, that the registry rescans published images, and that critical findings block promotion
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for container images are scanned before and after publish, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Base images are rebuilt and patched regularly
items.description: A defined cadence rebuilds images on updated bases so OS-level patches reach running workloads instead of lingering in stale layers. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Supply Chain Security — Base Image Security: Verify a rebuild/patch cadence exists, that stale images are flagged, and that patched bases propagate to downstream images
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for base images are rebuilt and patched regularly, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

sections.title: Infrastructure as Code — IaC Misconfiguration Scanning

items.title: IaC is scanned for misconfigurations in CI
items.description: Terraform, CloudFormation, and Kubernetes manifests are scanned on every change for insecure defaults before infrastructure is provisioned. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Infrastructure as Code — IaC Misconfiguration Scanning: Confirm IaC scanning runs on all infra repos in CI, covers the IaC formats in use, and fails on high-severity misconfigurations
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for iac is scanned for misconfigurations in ci, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Kubernetes manifests are checked against hardening guidance
items.description: K8s manifests and Helm charts are scanned for insecure settings (privileged containers, host mounts, missing limits) against CIS Kubernetes guidance. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Infrastructure as Code — IaC Misconfiguration Scanning: Verify manifest scanning flags privileged/hostPath/root settings and that critical issues are remediated before apply
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for kubernetes manifests are checked against hardening guidance, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: IaC is validated against cloud security baselines
items.description: Scanning rules map to cloud provider security baselines (encryption at rest, public exposure, logging) so provisioned resources meet a known bar. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Infrastructure as Code — IaC Misconfiguration Scanning: Confirm rule sets align to CIS cloud benchmarks, cover encryption and public-access checks, and exceptions are documented
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for iac is validated against cloud security baselines, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: IaC scan results are tracked and not bypassed
items.description: Findings flow into tracking with owners, and suppressions are explicit and auditable rather than silently disabling rules. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find: Infrastructure as Code — IaC Misconfiguration Scanning: Verify findings are tracked to remediation, that rule suppressions are justified in code, and bypass requires approval
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for iac scan results are tracked and not bypassed, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

sections.title: Infrastructure as Code — Policy as Code

items.title: Security guardrails are codified as policy
items.description: Organizational security policies (no public buckets, required tags, mandatory encryption) are expressed as code and evaluated automatically. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Infrastructure as Code — Policy as Code: Confirm critical guardrails exist as policy, are version-controlled, and are evaluated in CI against IaC plans
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for security guardrails are codified as policy, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Admission control enforces policy at deploy time
items.description: A policy engine in the cluster (or cloud) rejects non-compliant resources at admission, providing a runtime backstop beyond pipeline checks. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Infrastructure as Code — Policy as Code: Verify admission policies are enforced (not audit-only) for critical controls and that violations are denied and logged
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for admission control enforces policy at deploy time, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Policies are unit-tested and reviewed
items.description: Policy code has its own tests and change review so guardrails behave as intended and cannot be weakened without scrutiny. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Infrastructure as Code — Policy as Code: Confirm policies have test cases, changes go through review, and policy repos are protected from unauthorized modification
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for policies are unit-tested and reviewed, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Policy coverage spans pipeline and runtime
items.description: The same intent is enforced both in CI (fast feedback) and at runtime (authoritative), avoiding gaps where one layer is the only control. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Infrastructure as Code — Policy as Code: Verify key policies are enforced in both CI and admission/runtime, and that any single-layer gaps are intentional and risk-accepted
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for policy coverage spans pipeline and runtime, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Security-relevant events are logged centrally
items.description: Authentication, authorization, and administrative actions are logged to a central, tamper-resistant store with sufficient retention. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Runtime & Monitoring — Security Observability: Confirm security events are centrally aggregated, logs are protected from tampering, and retention meets policy and compliance needs
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for security-relevant events are logged centrally, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: OWASP Top Ten — Security Logging and Monitoring Failures

items.title: Actionable security alerts are configured
items.description: Detections for suspicious behavior (brute force, privilege escalation, anomalous egress) generate alerts routed to an owner with a response path. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Runtime & Monitoring — Security Observability: Verify alert rules exist for key threat scenarios, route to an on-call owner, and are tuned to limit noise while catching real events
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for actionable security alerts are configured, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: OWASP Top Ten — Security Logging and Monitoring Failures

items.title: Runtime threat detection is deployed for workloads
items.description: Runtime sensors detect anomalous container/host behavior (unexpected process execution, file tampering, suspicious syscalls) in production. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Runtime & Monitoring — Security Observability: Confirm a runtime detection agent is deployed across workloads, rules cover common attack behaviors, and detections feed alerting
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for runtime threat detection is deployed for workloads, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Configuration drift is detected and reconciled
items.description: Running infrastructure is continuously compared to its declared IaC state so manual or unauthorized changes are surfaced and reconciled. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Runtime & Monitoring — Security Observability: Verify drift detection runs against production, surfaces out-of-band changes, and that drift is alerted on or auto-reconciled
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for configuration drift is detected and reconciled, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

sections.title: Runtime & Monitoring — Remediation SLAs & Feedback Loop

items.title: Vulnerability remediation SLAs are defined and tracked
items.description: Time-to-remediate targets are set by severity and tracked, so critical vulnerabilities are fixed within a bounded, measurable window. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Runtime & Monitoring — Remediation SLAs & Feedback Loop: Confirm SLAs exist per severity, are tracked with dashboards, and that SLA breaches are escalated rather than silently aging
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for vulnerability remediation slas are defined and tracked, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Production signals feed back into the pipeline
items.description: Incidents, runtime detections, and pentest findings generate new pipeline checks, threat-model updates, or guidance so the same class does not recur. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Runtime & Monitoring — Remediation SLAs & Feedback Loop: Verify a mechanism converts recurring findings/incidents into new automated tests or controls, and that closure is tracked
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for production signals feed back into the pipeline, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: A vulnerability disclosure intake exists
items.description: External researchers have a clear, monitored channel to report vulnerabilities, and reports are triaged into the same remediation workflow. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find: Runtime & Monitoring — Remediation SLAs & Feedback Loop: Confirm a published disclosure policy/contact exists, reports are acknowledged within a target time, and feed the tracked SLA workflow
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for a vulnerability disclosure intake exists, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Program metrics drive continuous improvement
items.description: Trend metrics (mean time to remediate, escape rate, coverage) are reviewed regularly to steer investment and demonstrate program maturity. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find: Runtime & Monitoring — Remediation SLAs & Feedback Loop: Verify a metric set is collected, reviewed on a cadence with stakeholders, and used to prioritize security program improvements
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for program metrics drive continuous improvement, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.
